narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on academic and background factors from past students. The model estimated a 72% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which is high enough to trigger an automatic rejection of your admission to protect the school's outcomes and bar passage rates. The primary reason for this prediction centers on your school decile rankings from earlier years, which stood out as the strongest influences pushing the model toward expecting failure. Specifically, your Year 3 decile of 2—well below the typical student's 5.5—had the biggest negative impact, signaling to the model that your performance in that critical later stage was weaker than most applicants who succeed on the bar. Your Year 1 decile of 3, also lower than the average around 5.7, reinforced this concern as the second-most influential factor, suggesting early academic struggles that carried weight in the prediction. On a more positive note, your LSAT score of 30.5 pulled the prediction somewhat toward success, counteracting the other signals since it's a key indicator of legal reasoning ability, even if below the dataset average of 36.7. While your undergraduate GPA of 3.3 exceeds the typical 3.2, and your middle family income bracket aligns with many students, these didn't outweigh the top concerns from your school deciles. Being in the full-time track, like over 90% of students, was neutral and expected. Compared to typical admitted students, who often have higher decile rankings in later years and stronger overall academic momentum, your profile appeared riskier for bar passage despite some solid elements like your undergrad grades. This isn't a judgment on your potential as an Asian male applicant—many from similar backgrounds excel—but the model focused heavily on those decile patterns as harbingers of bar challenges. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your school deciles, aiming for top-5 rankings, and perhaps preparing intensively for a higher LSAT retake to shift future predictions. You could also seek targeted bar prep resources or admissions appeals highlighting any extenuating circumstances from those early years. With focused improvements, reapplying next cycle could yield a very different outcome.
